behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is a deterministic backoff calculation at a single logic boundary; no OS/network/subprocess or external side effect is required, and no AI/ML output is involved. Higher levels were considered and rejected because the behavior text is only about the doubling rule and cap, not module handoff or real HTTP behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Unit-test the backoff function/state with consecutive failure counts and a configured max, asserting delays follow 1x/2x/4x... and clamp at the maximum.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: Failure risk is in module interaction: transport error mapping + rate-limit classifier + backoff computation. This is beyond one pure function, so unit-only can miss handoff bugs. System was considered, but rejected because behavior text does not require validating live third-party response contracts; in-process stubbed HTTP responses can exercise this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with a stub transport returning a synthetic rate-limit response (e.g., 429 + retry metadata), and assert retry classifier flags it and computed next delay matches policy.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: This verifies orchestration across timer/backoff, retry loop, and request replay, which requires multiple modules wired together. No real infrastructure is strictly required; a deterministic in-process transport stub can prove resend semantics and returned value. System was rejected because behavior text does not require real external round-trip visibility beyond library boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test using fake timers and a stub transport sequence (rate-limited response then success); assert original request payload is resent, call count increments, and final returned result is the success payload.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: The typed error depends on retry counter state and terminal error mapping through the retry pipeline, which is cross-module behavior. Unit-only tests of the error class are insufficient to catch wiring mistakes. System was considered but rejected because live HTTP is not required to validate typed terminal error mapping.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with stub transport that always returns rate-limit failures; run until max retries and assert rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` with expected fields, not a generic error type.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is end-to-end library orchestration across retry state, backoff, and quota bookkeeping; unit-only misses emergent loop/termination bugs. No external OS/network/subprocess dependency is required to reveal this class of bug if timers and transport are controlled in-process. Workflow/Agentic do not apply (no LLM steps).
behaviors.test_description: Integration test with fake timers and a failing stub transport; assert bounded number of attempts, monotonic retry progression, and eventual surfaced terminal error within expected virtual time.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: This is shared-state/concurrency behavior across callers and quota manager modules; unit tests on isolated functions can miss race/order interactions. Real external infra is not strictly needed because same-process async concurrency can be reproduced in-process. System was considered but rejected because behavior text scopes to in-process callers and shared local bucket.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test launching concurrent `call()` invocations against a controlled stub transport; assert total permitted attempts follow one global quota bucket, not per-caller independent counters.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: FIFO dispatch correctness is queue-orchestration across async scheduling and dispatcher modules, not a single pure function in isolation. In-process concurrency/timer control is sufficient to expose ordering bugs. System was considered but rejected because real network/OS behavior is not required by the behavior text to validate submission-order dispatch.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test enqueuing multiple blocked requests, then releasing capacity; assert transport send order matches submission order under controlled timing.
